# Dalang SSO

> Dalang SSO is a self-hosted, open-source OAuth 2.0 / OpenID Connect identity provider written in Rust — a lightweight Keycloak alternative. It ships as a single ~7 MB static binary with an embedded SQLite database, works in airgapped environments, and provides client SDKs for JavaScript/TypeScript, Rust, Go, Python, Java and PHP. License: Apache-2.0.

## Summary

Dalang SSO lets you run your own "Sign in with…" on your own domain. Register OAuth clients in a dashboard to get a client_id and client_secret, then use the Authorization Code + PKCE flow. Key capabilities:

- OAuth 2.0 / OpenID Connect: authorization code (PKCE), refresh-token rotation, client credentials, discovery, JWKS, UserInfo.
- Multi-tenant with role-based access control: super (global), manager (own tenant: CRUD clients + secrets), developer (own tenant: rotate secrets only).
- Up to 2 secrets per client for zero-downtime rotation; per-client email allow-lists.
- Post-quantum option: ML-DSA (FIPS 204) token signing; classical RS256 default.
- Databases: SQLite (default), PostgreSQL, MySQL/MariaDB — selected at runtime via DATABASE_URL.
- Airgap-friendly: no CDN or external fonts; self-contained binary + embedded dashboard.

## Key endpoints

- GET /.well-known/openid-configuration — OIDC discovery
- GET /.well-known/jwks.json — public keys to verify tokens
- GET/POST /oauth/authorize — login + consent + authorization code
- POST /oauth/token — token endpoint (authorization_code, refresh_token, client_credentials)
- GET /oauth/userinfo — profile for a Bearer access token

## Docs

- Full documentation: https://sso.dalang.io/docs
- Quickstart, endpoints, SDKs, configuration, self-hosting, post-quantum

## Source & downloads

- Repository (Apache-2.0): https://github.com/dalang-io/sso
- Pre-built binaries: https://github.com/dalang-io/sso/releases
- Build from source: `cargo build --release -p sso-server` (Rust 1.90+)
- SDKs: https://github.com/dalang-io/sso/tree/main/sdks

## Comparison

Versus Keycloak: Dalang SSO is a single small Rust binary (not a JVM app), starts in milliseconds, defaults to embedded SQLite, has built-in post-quantum token signing, and is designed for airgapped deployment. It is leaner and does not aim to match every Keycloak feature (e.g. SAML, LDAP federation).
